text: This video describes two cases of invasive perianal Paget's disease treated with robotic abdominal perineal resection. The authors have no declarations.

The first case is a forty-eight year old obese female who had been treated for presumed chronic haemorrhoidal disease. Anal biopsies at EUA showed invasive adenocarcinoma with Pagetoids spread into the anal squamous epithelium.

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position with the right side down. The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the left mesocolon, exposing, ligating, and dividing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision down to the pelvic floor, halting dissection just proximal to the levators to prevent specimen coning. The proximal sigmoid colon is divided using an endoscopic stapler, and an end colostomy is matured. The abdominal incisions are then closed.

The patient is positioned in the Kraski position for a wide local excision of the perineum, with dissection maintained in the extra levator plane. The posterior vaginal wall is excised en bloc, and intraoperative frozen sections from the vaginal wall margins are sent for histopathology, all returning negative. The dissection includes all macroscopically abnormal skin and is deepened cephalad to connect with the abdominal dissection.
The surgeon reconstructs the posterior vaginal wall using interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures, followed by primary closure of the perineal defect.

The second case concerns a sixty-nine-year-old female with a past medical history of breast and endometrial cancer who underwent a colonoscopy. She was found to have anorectal adenocarcinoma with extensive perianal Paget's disease.

The robotic-assisted abdominoperineal resection (APR) is performed with a posterior vaginectomy. The surgeon uses a medial to lateral dissection to expose the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), which is ligated with clips and divided. A total mesorectal excision is carried out down to the level of the levators, and the bowel is divided using an endoscopic stapler.

The patient is positioned in the jackknife Kraski position for a wide perineal clearance, including the posterior vagina. The perineal defect is closed using a gluteal thigh flap, performed by the plastic surgeons.
The patient made an uneventful recovery and was discharged.

In conclusion, perianal Paget's disease is an intractable neoplastic disorder. that can be associated with other malignant diseases of the anal canal and colorectum. Colonoscopy should therefore be performed during the preoperative workup. Wide local excision of the skin lesion with clear margins is sufficient when invasive disease is absent. Invasive disease requires an extra levator, APR. The method of peroneal reconstruction depends upon the size of the defect and individual patient anatomical characteristics. Where primary closure is not possible, a gluteal thigh flap can be used for reconstruction. This is particularly well suited for minimally invasive surgery as an abdominal dissection is not required.
